text: Using a new fiber optic technique, surgeons can now perform sophisticated thoracic surgery through small incisions by first introducing a fiber optic scope that will display the internal anatomy on a TV screen.
A small incision is made so specialized surgical instruments may be introduced into the chest cavity.
This patient's lung, seen in the foreground, appears to be blackened from the long-term effects of smoking.

The thoroscopic procedure is useful for obtaining a tissue specimen or biopsy without entering the chest cavity with a larger conventional incision that would require a much longer recuperative period.
This procedure has also been used successfully in patients who suffer from chronic spontaneous pneumothorax or collapsed lung.
Lung blebs are weakened portions of the lung prone to rupture.
The blebs can be surgically removed in a procedure similar to the one shown here.
In this patient, a portion of his lung has been identified as cancerous.
In this procedure, the surgeon will remove the diseased portion of the lung using a stapling device introduced through an adjacent incision.
Bleeding can be easily controlled using an electrocautery.
This device cuts tissue and seals blood vessels on contact.
After the tumor has been freed from the lung, it is removed through the original chest incision.
